evals.prompt: Build me a HOT lead sequence for Priya Kapoor, VP of Engineering at Vantage Robotics (Series C, $85M raised, 320 employees, industrial automation for warehouse fulfillment). We sell FleetMind -- an AI-powered fleet orchestration platform that reduces warehouse robot downtime by 40%. Priya posted on LinkedIn last week about struggling with fleet coordination across their 3 new distribution centers. They also just hired a Head of Reliability Engineering, which signals they're investing in uptime. Our customer Apex Fulfillment (similar scale, same vertical) cut unplanned downtime from 11% to 3.2% in 90 days. Priya went to Georgia Tech, same as our CTO. Goal is to book a discovery call. Build the full 6-touch sequence.
evals.expected_output: A complete HOT lead outreach sequence for Priya Kapoor / Vantage Robotics with Lead tier: HOT, 6 touches over 21 days, goal of discovery call, and a mix of LinkedIn and email channels. Touch 1 (Day 1) should be a personalised first outreach referencing Priya's LinkedIn post about fleet coordination struggles and/or the reliability engineering hire. Touch 2 (Day 3) should use the alternate channel from Touch 1, reference the first outreach, and add a new angle (e.g., the Georgia Tech connection or the new distribution center expansion). Touch 3 (Day 7) should be a pure value-add with no ask -- sharing a relevant case study, industry stat, or framework about fleet coordination at scale. Touch 4 (Day 10) should reframe the problem with sector-specific insight (e.g., patterns in warehouse automation downtime) and include the Apex Fulfillment proof point (11% to 3.2% in 90 days), ending with the discovery call ask. Touch 5 (Day 17) should reference any new signal or do a respectful timing check. Touch 6 (Day 21) should be a short graceful close that leaves the door open with no ask and no guilt. Each touch should have full message text and word count. Branching rules section must include reply-exit rule, LinkedIn acceptance handling, email open acceleration, and bounce detection. Sequence completion section must include nurture cadence move and CRM logging.
expectations: To maximise response rate on the opening touch, Touch 1 leads with Priya Kapoor's specific situation (her LinkedIn post about fleet coordination across 3 distribution centers and/or the Head of Reliability Engineering hire) rather than leading with FleetMind or Vantage Robotics -- prospects respond to messages about their problem, not your product

To prevent the sequence from going stale, each of the 6 touches uses a distinct angle or proof point not present in any previous touch -- the available hooks (LinkedIn post, reliability hire, distribution center expansion, Georgia Tech connection, Apex Fulfillment case study, sector-level downtime patterns) must be distributed across touches, not front-loaded or repeated

To convert awareness into pipeline, Touch 4 introduces the Apex Fulfillment proof point (unplanned downtime from 11% to 3.2% in 90 days) as social proof from a similar-scale warehouse automation company -- same-vertical proof points are the highest-converting element in outreach sequences, and burying them in Touch 1 wastes their impact

To protect the relationship for future attempts, Touch 6 is a graceful close that is short, leaves the door open, contains no ask, and uses no guilt language or 'just checking in' -- a bad close poisons the entire account for re-engagement sequences later

To maintain channel effectiveness, at least 2 distinct channels (LinkedIn and email) are used across the 6 touches, and no single channel is used for consecutive touches within fewer than 3 days -- channel fatigue drives unsubscribes and connection-request rejections that permanently reduce reachability

To prevent live prospects from falling through cracks, branching rules include: reply at any touch exits the sequence and alerts the rep (continuing to sequence a prospect who replied destroys trust), and LinkedIn connection accepted without reply triggers a custom warm follow-up DM rather than the generic next sequence touch

evals.prompt: Need a WARM sequence for David Chen, Director of Revenue Operations at Lumen Health (mid-market healthtech SaaS, $28M ARR, 180 employees, sells EHR integration middleware to regional hospital networks). We sell DataBridge -- a RevOps data unification platform. David's company just appeared on a 'Top 50 Healthtech to Watch' list. His LinkedIn shows he joined Lumen 5 months ago from Salesforce where he was a Senior RevOps Analyst. They're hiring 3 SDRs and a Marketing Ops Manager according to their careers page -- signals a growth push. No direct interaction with David yet, but he liked a post from our VP Marketing about CRM data hygiene last month. We have a case study from MedSync (healthtech, similar ARR) that consolidated 4 data sources into one RevOps dashboard and improved forecast accuracy by 34%. Goal is to get a 20-minute intro call. Build the WARM 5-touch sequence.
evals.expected_output: A complete WARM lead outreach sequence for David Chen / Lumen Health with Lead tier: WARM, 5 touches over 35 days, goal of 20-minute intro call, and a mix of LinkedIn and email channels. Touch 1 (Day 1) should be an email with lighter personalisation than HOT but still referencing something specific (the Top 50 list, his background at Salesforce, or the hiring signals). Touch 2 (Day 7) should be LinkedIn value-add (e.g., sharing a relevant insight about RevOps scaling during growth phases). Touch 3 (Day 14) should be an email with a problem insight and no pitch -- addressing a challenge relevant to his situation (scaling RevOps data with a growing team, forecast accuracy with multiple data sources). Touch 4 (Day 28) should be email or LinkedIn with a soft check-in, possibly referencing the MedSync case study as social proof. Touch 5 (Day 35) should be a graceful close. Each touch should have full message text and word count. Branching rules and sequence completion sections must be present.
expectations: To earn a response from a WARM lead with no prior interaction, early touches (1-2) reference David Chen's specific situation -- the Top 50 Healthtech list, his move from Salesforce 5 months ago, the SDR and Marketing Ops hiring signals, or his engagement with the VP Marketing CRM data hygiene post -- rather than leading with DataBridge or generic healthtech pain points

To build credibility before asking, Touch 3 introduces a problem insight relevant to David's situation (scaling RevOps data across a growing team, forecast accuracy with multiple data sources) with no pitch and no ask -- WARM leads who receive value before the ask convert to meetings at higher rates than those who get pitched early

To deploy social proof at peak impact, the MedSync case study (4 data sources consolidated, 34% forecast accuracy improvement, similar healthtech ARR) appears in Touch 4 rather than Touch 1 -- same-vertical proof points land harder after the prospect has context on the problem being solved

To protect the relationship for future re-engagement, Touch 5 is a graceful close that is short, respectful, leaves the door open, contains no ask, and does not use 'just checking in' or pressuring language -- a WARM lead who gets a respectful close is 3x more likely to respond to a re-engagement sequence 90 days later

To prevent channel fatigue over the 35-day WARM cadence, touches use at least 2 distinct channels (email and LinkedIn) and no channel is used for consecutive touches within a 3-day window -- WARM sequences have longer spacing, making channel variety even more important for staying visible without being annoying

To prevent a responding prospect from receiving automated follow-ups that destroy trust, branching rules include a reply-exit rule (any reply at any touch exits the sequence and alerts the rep), and the sequence completion section includes moving non-responders to a cultivate nurture cadence with CRM logging

evals.prompt: I need a re-engagement sequence. We had a prospect, Maria Santos, Head of Partnerships at NovaTrade (B2B marketplace for cross-border commodity trading, Series B, $42M raised, 210 employees). She took a demo with our AE back in October 2025 and said she loved the product but 'timing wasn't right -- we're mid-migration to a new ERP and can't take on another integration until Q1.' It's now March 2026. Since our last conversation: NovaTrade announced they completed their ERP migration in January (press release), they expanded into 3 new LATAM markets last month, and Maria was promoted from Senior Partnerships Manager to Head of Partnerships. We sell TradeLink -- a partner ecosystem management platform. Our customer PortBridge (similar B2B marketplace, cross-border) increased partner-sourced revenue by 28% in 6 months after deploying TradeLink. Build the 3-touch re-engagement sequence to restart the conversation and see if timing has changed.
evals.expected_output: A complete RE-ENGAGEMENT outreach sequence for Maria Santos / NovaTrade with Lead tier: RE-ENGAGE, 3 touches over 21 days, goal of re-engagement / timing check, and a mix of channels. Touch 1 (Day 1) should specifically reference the October demo and her stated blocker (ERP migration), acknowledge the blocker is now resolved (January press release), and reconnect with a reason tied to the changed circumstances. Touch 2 (Day 10) should introduce new value -- something relevant that has changed (LATAM expansion creating new partnership complexity, Maria's promotion meaning bigger mandate, or the PortBridge case study showing 28% partner revenue lift). Touch 3 (Day 21) should be a graceful close or hand-off. Each touch should have full message text and word count. Branching rules and sequence completion sections must be present.
expectations: To reactivate a warm relationship rather than starting cold, Touch 1 specifically references the October 2025 demo and the stated blocker (ERP migration), then acknowledges the blocker's resolution (completed in January per press release) as the reason for reconnecting -- re-engagement sequences that open cold as if no prior relationship exists get response rates no better than cold outreach

To create urgency tied to Maria Santos's changed circumstances, Touch 2 introduces new value from at least one of: the LATAM expansion creating new partnership complexity across 3 markets, Maria's promotion from Senior Partnerships Manager to Head of Partnerships giving her a bigger mandate, or the PortBridge case study (28% partner-sourced revenue increase in 6 months) -- each of these is a distinct buying signal that did not exist during the October demo

To prevent burning a prospect who already expressed product interest, Touch 3 is a graceful close or hand-off that is short, respectful, and leaves the door open without pressuring or guilting Maria into responding -- she said she loved the product, so a bad close risks permanently losing a prospect who is already sold on value

To reach Maria through different attention windows, at least 2 distinct channels are used across the 3 touches (not all 3 on the same channel) -- re-engagement prospects are harder to reach because they already filtered you out once, so channel variety is essential for breaking through

To ensure each touch earns its place in the sequence, no two touches repeat identical content -- Touch 1 anchors on the prior relationship and resolved blocker, Touch 2 introduces new value (LATAM expansion, promotion, or PortBridge proof point), and Touch 3 provides the graceful close -- a 3-touch sequence has zero margin for repetition

To prevent a re-engaged prospect from getting automated messages after replying, branching rules include the reply-exit rule (any reply at any touch exits the sequence and alerts the rep), and the sequence completion section specifies moving to a cultivate nurture cadence if no response after all 3 touches -- re-engaged prospects who reply need immediate human follow-up, not another automated touch
